This report is one of several reports which maps the strategy for the future use and disposition of uranium-233 $(^{233}\mathrm{U})$ and disposal of wastes containing $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ .
- ORNL/TM-13550-Strategy for the Future Use and Disposition of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ : Overview.
This document is a summary of the path forward for disposition of surplus $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ .
- ORNL/TM-13517-Definition of Weapons Usable $^{233}U$ .
This document determines and defines how much DU must be mixed with $^{233}U$ to convert the $^{233}U$ into a non-weapons-usable material.
- ORNL/TM-13600-Technical Handbook of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ Material Properties, Processing, and Handling Guidelines.
This document is a reference handbook for handling and processing $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ .
Weapons-usable fissile materials include plutonium, high-enriched uranium (HEU), and uranium-233 $\left({}^{233}\mathrm{U}\right)$ .
In support of this program, Oak Ridge National Laboratory led DOE's contractor efforts to identify and characterize options for the long-term storage and disposal of excess ${}^{233}\mathrm{U}$ .
Five storage and 17 disposal options were identified and are described herein.

Storage, as a type of disposition, refers to methods to isotopically dilute $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ with depleted uranium (DU) to convert the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ into a non-weapons-usable form suitable for long-term storage or future use.

Disposal, as a type of disposition, refers to processing the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ and disposing of the material as a waste in a manor that makes the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ inaccessible and unattractive for use in nuclear weapons.
The characteristics of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ are different from those of HEU or plutonium.
Consequently, the best options for the storage and disposal of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ may be different from those for HEU and plutonium.
Uranium-233 has the chemical characteristics of uranium, but it is an alpha emitter like plutonium and thus, as a minimum, must be processed within an alpha containment (e.g., in a glovebox).
Uranium-233 can be rendered unsuitable for weapons use by isotopically diluting it in $^{238}\mathrm{U}$ to $< 12$ wt %.
It could be further diluted with $^{238}\mathrm{U}$ to minimize nuclear criticality issues associated with waste disposal.
Uranium-233 has a characteristic that makes most plutonium or HEU disposition options not viable for $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ .
In the production of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ , some $^{232}\mathrm{U}$ is produced.
The $^{232}\mathrm{U}$ has a decay product, thallium-208 ( $^{208}\mathrm{Tl}$ ), which decays to lead and produces high-energy, 2.6-MeV gamma rays.
The concentration of $^{232}\mathrm{U}$ determines the radiation shielding required to protect workers.
Uranium-233, which contains very low levels $[<1$ part per million (ppm)] of $^{232}\mathrm{U}$ , has correspondingly low levels of gamma radiation.

Uranium-233 with higher concentrations of $^{232}\mathrm{U}$ (greater than a few ppm) and with associated radioactive decay products requires heavy radiation shielding and remote-handling (RH) operations to protect workers from gamma radiation.
Most facilities designed to process HEU or plutonium are unsuitable to process $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ .
The CEUSP $^{233}U$ inventory contains $\sim 1,040$ kg of uranium in $\sim 400$ containers.
The uranium isotopic composition is $\sim 10$ wt $\%^{233}U$ , 76 wt $\%^{235}U$ , and a complex mixture of other uranium isotopes.
It contains high concentrations of $^{232}U$ that create a significant radiation field.
LWBR $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ refers to a collection of unirradiated nuclear fuel.

The LWBR $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ inventory contains $\sim 350\mathrm{kg}$ of $^{233}\mathrm{UO}_2$ which is combined with $\sim 14,000\mathrm{kg}$ of thorium oxide $(\mathrm{ThO}_2)$ in the form of unirradiated high-fired ceramic reactor fuel.
The uranium isotopic composition is $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ with low levels of $^{232}\mathrm{U}$ .
The presence of the $\mathrm{ThO}_2$ makes chemical processing of the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ difficult.
The $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ could be stored for future use.
There is current interest in production of bismuth-213 ( $^{213}\mathrm{Bi}$ ) from $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ for treatment of certain cancers.
Bismuth-213 is a decay product of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ .
Uranium-233 is an alpha emitter like plutonium; thus, the most appropriate disposal facility to use is a geological repository.
Uranium-233 waste forms could be accepted at YM under several sets of conditions, provided that they are in acceptable chemical and physical forms.
The WIPP site would be technically acceptable for disposal of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ wastes, but the WIPP enabling legislation does not authorize disposal of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ wastes at this site.
However, TRUW containing $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ is allowed.

Existing $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ wastes and other wastes that require a WIPP-type disposal facility, but which can not be sent to WIPP, are packaged to meet WIPP WACs because these criteria define not only the requirements for waste acceptance at WIPP but also those for long-term storage, transportation, and disposal of such wastes at future WIPP-type facilities.

For a $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ disposition option that generates WIPP-type wastes, there are two suboptions: (1) send the material to WIPP after appropriate changes in laws or regulations or (2) add the material to the existing $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ wastes in inventory.

The volumes of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ wastes in existing storage facilities are significantly larger than the potential volumes of wastes from any $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ disposition option; thus, the addition of wastes from $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ disposition activities would not fundamentally alter future $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ waste management operations.
Storage of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ as a non-weapons-usable waste is significantly less expensive with lower accompanying risks than storing weapons-usable $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ .
The $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ storage and disposal options are shown in Table ES.1.

For example, aqueous processing may be used to (1) recover medical isotopes from $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ , (2) down Blend $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ with $^{238}\mathrm{U}$ to non-weapons-usable material for long-term storage and future use, or (3) convert the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ into a form for disposal.

Conversion of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ into a form for disposal usually includes down-blending the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ with $^{238}\mathrm{U}$ to minimize safeguards and repository nuclear criticality issues.
The technical viability of each option for each type of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ is indicated.
Some options are suitable for only certain categories of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ .
For example, the uranium-aluminum melt-dilute option (described below) can be used to process clean and CEUSP $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ , but not LWBR $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ .
Uranium-233 could be disposed of by mixing the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ with existing HLW sludges containing DU and converting the mixture into HLW glass for disposal at YM.
The option is technically applicable to all categories of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ in inventory.
The viability of the option for managing LWBR $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ is unclear because of several technical factors.
It is uncertain whether the SRS tanks have sufficient DU to address criticality issues if the entire inventory of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ was disposed of.

In the aqueous-nitrate-blending process, solid $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ material would be dissolved in nitric acid to produce an aqueous, uranyl-nitrate solution, which is mixed with a uranyl-nitrate solution of DU.
The process would be applicable to all $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ in inventory.
If the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ is to be disposed of as waste, it could be sent to a YM- or WIPP-type repository.
Several DOE facilities, with modifications, could process all $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ .

In the fusion-melt blend process, the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ oxide would be mixed with DU oxide powder and solvent metal oxide powder and then melted in an induction furnace to produce a glasslike material.
All categories of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ can be processed.
The option allows for the future recovery of medical isotopes or the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ for other uses.
Clean and CEUSP $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ can be processed; LWBR $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ probably can not be processed because of the chemical effects of thorium on glass properties.
The process would not be used for storage of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ for future use because of the difficulty of recovering the uranium or medical isotopes from the glass.

Uranium-233 could be disposed of by mixing it with other oxides, converting the mixture into a ceramic form, and packaging the mixture in small cans.
The cans would be placed inside an empty HLW canister, and HLW glass would be poured around the cans containing $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ .

There are institutional advantages for using the same processes proposed for plutonium disposition or for modifying the plutonium disposition process to permit the inclusion of selected inventories of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ material as a feed stream.

The $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ has higher gamma-radiation levels than does plutonium; however, the LWBR $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ could probably be co-processed with the plutonium with minor modification of the plutonium immobilization facility.
The thorium in the LWBR $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ could replace some or all of the functions of the uranium in the final product.
The clean and CEUSP $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ radiation levels are too high to allow processing in the plutonium immobilization facility.
Some RH TRUW processes, such as plasma processes, can co-process $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ , DU, and RH TRUW into a form acceptable for WIPP while isotopically blending the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ and DU.
Depending upon future waste management decisions, options may exist to dispose of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ via this route.
In this option, the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ would be co-processed with CH TRUW and DU to produce a waste acceptable for disposal at WIPP.
The $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ would be isotopically blended with the DU.
The process is applicable to $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ inventories with low external radiation levels-primarily the LWBR $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ .
It may not be viable for $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ materials with higher radiation levels, such as the CEUSP material.
- The option can be used to process only $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ with low gamma-radiation levels.
The LWBR $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ material may meet this requirement.
It is relatively pure $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ and is mixed with 14 t of $\mathrm{ThO}_2$ .
- The CH TRUW facility must use a process that isotopically blends $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ with DU.
The treatment process has been changed, and the new process no longer assures isotopic blending of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ with DU.
The reactor option would convert the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ into LWR fuel for irradiation in power reactors.
This option would allow the beneficial use of the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ and would produce an SNF which would be acceptable for disposal at YM.
The $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ would be blended with DU to make a LEU oxide feed for the fuel.
The Shippingport reactor operated using only $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ as a fuel.
This option would use a disposition path similar to that used to disposition the HEU-blend the fissile material to $< 5\mathrm{wt}\%$ as feed to a nuclear fuel fabrication plant.
The production of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ nuclear fuels would require special process facilities for uranium purification, conversion to uranium dioxide, and fabrication of nuclear fuel.
Neither existing $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ or proposed plutonium fuel fabrication facilities could process $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ because of the radiation fields of the U.S. $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ inventory.

The aqueous processing option for $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ disposition, described earlier, is effectively the first step in a multistep process to make nuclear fuel, which requires the additional steps of uranium purification, fuel fabrication, and fuel irradiation.

There is a batch of material that contains $42.6\mathrm{kg}$ of uranium consisting of $0.8\mathrm{kg}$ of very pure ${}^{233}\mathrm{U}$ and $38.7\mathrm{kg}$ of ${}^{235}\mathrm{U}$ at the Y-12 plant.

The low concentration of the ${}^{233}\mathrm{U}$ , the lack of impurities in this ${}^{233}\mathrm{U}$ , and its alloying with ${}^{235}\mathrm{U}$ may allow disposition of this material by converting this material to LWR nuclear fuel.
Uranium-233 could be converted to an appropriate form and disposed of in boreholes at depths of several kilometers.
Uranium-233 could be converted to an appropriate form and disposed of in GCD disposal facilities.
A GCD facility disposes of waste at somewhat greater depths than does a shallow-land disposal facility-typically 10 to $30\mathrm{m}$ underground.
Uranium-233 could be disposed of by launching it into space or the sun or placing it into high earth or solar orbit.
This would not be a viable option for disposal of the small inventory of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ .
Uranium-233 could be disposed of by placing it under the ocean seabed (1) in the mid-ocean or (2) under a small oceanic island.
It would be a very expensive stand-alone option for disposal of the small inventory of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ .
The $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ could be diluted by a factor of $\sim 100,000$ to meet the definition of low-level waste and then disposed of in shallow-land disposal facilities.
The $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ could be downblended with DU using the electrometallurgical process that produces a uranium metal product.
Seventeen methods to disposition weapons-usable uranium-233 $(^{233}\mathrm{U})$ were identified and characterized.
Some methods isotopically dilute $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ with depleted uranium to convert it to non-weapons-usable uranium.
Other methods make the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ chemically or physically inaccessible.

Storage, in the context of this report, refers to (1) isotopically down-blending weapons-usable $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ with $^{238}\mathrm{U}$ to convert it to nonweapons-usable $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ and (2) placing the material in long-term storage.
Future down-blended $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ could be used for certain nondefense applications or be disposed of as a waste.
Disposal refers to all of the steps necessary to permanently dispose of the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ in a way that makes the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ inaccessible and unattractive for use in nuclear weapons.

However, the preferred technology(ies) for storage may be different from those for disposal because the requirements for long-term storage (with potential use of down-blended $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ ) are different from the requirements for ultimate disposal of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ in a repository.
Multiple unique institutional issues are associated with $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ .
Uranium-233 is a minor fissile material; that is, the quantities of this material are small compared to those of HEU and WGP.
Because $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ was never used in the United States on a large scale, the institutional structure that exists for HEU and WGP does not exist for $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ .
An institutional structure will have to be created for the disposition of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ .
Uranium-233 is chemically identical to natural and enriched uranium (Bereolos April 1998).
The same chemical processes used for depleted uranium (DU), natural uranium, and HEU are usually applicable to $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ .
However, as a radionuclide, $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ has a higher specific radioactivity than does DU, natural uranium, or HEU.
Consequently, certain radiation-induced chemical reactions occur faster in uranium containing significant quantities of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ when compared to reactions involving other uranium isotopes.

This phenomenon is important in certain situations such as long-term storage during which the higher radiation levels of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ require that storage containers and $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ storage forms do not contain organics (plastics etc.) or water that degrades and forms deleterious gases at higher radiation levels.

However, neither U.S. nor international regulations explicitly address the required isotopic dilution of ${}^{233}\mathrm{U}$ with ${}^{238}\mathrm{U}$ to convert ${}^{233}\mathrm{U}$ to nonweapons-usable ${}^{233}\mathrm{U}$ .
In the production of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ , some $^{232}\mathrm{U}$ is produced.
The concentrations of $^{232}\mathrm{U}$ depend upon the specifics of the production techniques for $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ .
The $^{232}\mathrm{U}$ has a decay product, thallium-208 ( $^{208}\mathrm{Tl}$ ), which decays to lead and produces a high-energy, 2.6-MeV gamma ray.
The concentration of $^{232}\mathrm{U}$ determines the radiation shielding required to protect workers.
Superior-quality $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ contains very low levels [~1 part per million (ppm)] of $^{232}\mathrm{U}$ and has correspondingly low levels of gamma radiation.

Low-quality $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ with higher concentrations of $^{232}\mathrm{U}$ (greater than a few ppm) and associated radioactive decay products requires heavy radiation shielding and remote-handling (RH) operations to protect workers from gamma radiation (Appendix A).
The $^{232}\mathrm{U}$ in low-quality $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ also impacts the requirements of off-gas systems for processing these materials.
Uranium-232 decays through several isotopes to the noble gas $^{220}\mathrm{Rn}$ .
Radon-220 further decays to $^{208}\mathrm{Tl}$ -the radionuclide with the 2.6-MeV gamma ray.
The $^{220}\mathrm{Rn}$ , as an inert gas, can pass through high-efficiency particulate air (HEPA) filters and then decay to $^{208}\mathrm{Tl}$ .

- Following the first filter, the system must contain charcoal beds, delay lines ( $\sim 10$ min), or other special equipment to hold the radon in the off-gas system until it decays to a solid material that can be removed from the off-gas with a second HEPA filter.
A second HEPA filter catches the solid decay products of $^{220}\mathrm{Rn}$ including $^{208}\mathrm{Tl}$ .

If $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ is chemically purified with removal of the decay products, the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ with significant concentrations of $^{232}\mathrm{U}$ can be processed and converted into desired forms in gloveboxes and other enclosures without significant radiation exposure to workers.

It takes time (days to weeks) for the $^{232}\mathrm{U}$ radioactive decay products that emit gamma rays to build up to high enough concentrations such as to require thick radiation shielding to protect the workers.
If $^{233}\mathrm{U}-^{232}\mathrm{U}$ contamination remains in the system, radiation levels will build up with time and can dominate the radiation field from such processes.

The radiological characteristics of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ have historically determined what uranium was to be managed as $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ .
If a mixture of uranium contains several isotopes, the mixture is handled as $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ provided that the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ is the primary hazard.
In practice, this procedure implies that uranium materials containing somewhat $>1$ wt % $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ would be handled as $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ .
Although $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ has been investigated for many applications, it has not been used on a large scale in the United States.
The technical characteristics of the WIPP make it suitable for disposal of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ wastes, but current law does not consider $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ wastes in the WIPP enabling legislation.
Thus, wastes containing only $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ can not legally go to WIPP.
To dispose of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ materials, a set of institutional issues that are unique to $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ must be addressed.
The quantities of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ are sufficiently small such that it would be cost-prohibitive to build special facilities and to develop a separate institutional framework for this material.
Many of the institutional structures developed for HEU and WGP can be modified and extended to $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ .
As a fissile material, $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ is similar to WGP.
The IAEA (IAEA August 1993) defines Category I quantities of weapons-usable materials as $2\mathrm{kg}$ of WGP, $2\mathrm{kg}$ of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ , and $5\mathrm{kg}$ of HEU.
For $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ , the IAEA regulations (August 1993) do not recognize that mixing $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ with DU will create a mixture that is unsuitable for the manufacture of nuclear weapons.

It is widely recognized within the technical community that isotopic dilution with DU will eliminate $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ as a weapons-usable material; however, all $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ -bearing materials containing significant quantities of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ are treated as weapons-usable material.
Historically, there never was any serious consideration of converting $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ to a non-weapons-usable material; thus, the required regulatory structure was not established.

The technical basis for converting $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ to non-weapons-usable material by diluting it with $^{238}\mathrm{U}$ is understood, but the regulations and other institutional agreements are not in place.

Because one of the goals of the Fissile Materials Disposition Program is to reduce the risks from these materials in other countries, institutional agreements as to the level of isotopic dilution that eliminates the weapons potential of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ are required (Forsberg et al.

The isotopic purity that renders $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ non-weapons-usable ( $<12\mathrm{wt\%}^{233}\mathrm{U}$ in $^{238}\mathrm{U}$ ) is less than that for HEU ( $<20\mathrm{wt\%}^{235}\mathrm{U}$ in $^{238}\mathrm{U}$ ).
In the United States, no facilities currently exist for disposing of wastes containing significant concentrations of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ .

Historically, wastes containing $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ have been managed as TRUWs (i.e., managed similarly to wastes containing plutonium) because the primary hazard-alpha radiation-is identical in both waste types; thus, the same types of disposal facilities are required.
However, the enabling legislation does not allow the disposal of wastes containing only $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ or wastes from nondefense activities.
DOE has several thousand drums of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ -containing wastes stored at ORNL, Idaho National Engineering and Environmental Laboratory (INEEL), and Los Alamos National Laboratory.
A WIPP-type facility would be suitable for $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ waste disposal.

From the perspectives of long-term storage and disposition options, the separated inventory can be divided into three major categories: clean, Consolidated Edison Uranium Solidification Program (CEUSP), and light-water breeder reactor (LWBR) $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ .
Because of technical, cost, and other factors, many options can only manage one or two of these $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ categories.

Figure 2.3 shows these categories where the area on top of the cylinder represents the quantity of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ and the volume represents the total volume of material ( $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ and other materials).
Table 2.2 further breaks down the 3 major categories into subcategories.
More detailed inventory information is available in a companion report (Bereolos June 1998).
Uranium-233 in spent nuclear fuel (SNF) and irradiated targets is not included in these numbers.
The inventory contains $\sim 800\mathrm{kg}^{233}\mathrm{U}$ in $\sim 1,800\mathrm{kg}$ of uranium in a total of 1,505 packages at multiple sites.
Most of the separated $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ and most of the packages are located at ORNL in the National Repository for $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ .
The $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ is typically packaged in double containers with the inner container made of stainless steel or aluminum.
Figure 2.4 shows the primary types of storage containers.
Figure 2.5 shows the chemical compositions of the three categories.
In addition to the separated $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ , there is a significant inventory of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ in SNF (Table 2.3).
The clean $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ consists of five large batches of material plus many small lots.
The uranium isotopic composition is $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ with variable impurity levels of $^{232}\mathrm{U}$ that are measured in ppm (Table 2.2).
The radiation levels vary widely depending upon the $^{232}\mathrm{U}$ content.
The CEUSP $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ was created from the irradiation of a HEU-thorium fuel in the Indian Point Reactor Unit I, which is owned by the Consolidation Edison Company.
The SNF was reprocessed with the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ shipped in the form of a uranium-nitrate aqueous solution to ORNL, where it was solidified for storage.
First, the CEUSP materials is a mixture of $\sim 10$ wt $\%$ $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ , $\sim 76$ wt $\%$ $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ , and other uranium isotopes.
It is $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ isotopically diluted with HEU.

This report describes storage and disposal options for $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ .

For $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ , the regulations (IAEA August 1993) do not recognize that mixing $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ with DU will create a mixture that is unsuitable for manufacturing nuclear weapons.
It is recognized within the technical community that isotopic dilution with DU will eliminate the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ as a weapons-usable material.

This concentration is equivalent to uranium with a $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ content $< 20$ wt % in $^{238}\mathrm{U}$ .

The $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ can be isotopically diluted to convert it to non-weapons-usable $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ and to eliminate the potential for long-term nuclear criticality.

The required isotopic dilution is $< 0.66\mathrm{wt}\%^{233}\mathrm{U}$ in $^{238}\mathrm{U}$ to eliminate the potential of nuclear criticality and essentially renders the mixture unusable for all potential applications.
It becomes unusable because of (1) isotopic impurities or (2) the prohibitive cost of processing the bulk of the material to access some desirable decay product.
This is a distinct and separate option from use options (keep as weapons-usable material or convert to non-weapons-usable $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ ) or the disposal options.
It is potentially viable because DOE has a significant inventory of existing $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ wastes (Forsberg, Storch, and Lewis July 7, 1998) with no defined disposal site.

Consequently, adding a few hundred additional drums of waste of isotopically diluted $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ to this existing inventory of several thousand drums would not significantly increase the waste management activities or costs.

The option (1) does allow the conversion of weapons-usable $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ into a non-weapons-usable $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ -containing waste; (2) reduces storage costs because of a lessened need for security requirements; and (3) does not create new waste management problems.

The $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ isotope is used as a calibration spike in the determination of uranium concentrations and isotopic compositions in materials containing natural uranium or uranium enriched in $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ .
While the quantities of material used are very small (typically fractions of a gram), pure $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ is desired for such applications.
The major historical application for $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ has been for research into new nuclear power reactors and associated fuel cycles.
There are four incentives for considering a $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ -thorium fuel cycle.

- SNF and other wastes from the thorium- $^{233}$ U fuel cycle, when compared to uranium-plutonium fuel cycles, contain far smaller quantities of long-lived actinides that are a concern in wastes to be disposed in geological repositories.

Nuclear criticality can be prevented by controlling equipment and facility geometry, limiting the quantity of fissile material in the system, adding neutron absorbers, or isotopically diluting $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ with DU.

Nuclear criticality avoidance by isotopic dilution is the addition of $^{238}\mathrm{U}$ sufficient such as to lower the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ purity level below that at which nuclear criticality can occur.
Isotopic dilution with DU is a preferred criticality control option for many $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ disposition options based on the following considerations:
For relatively small quantities of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ , strong economic incentives exist to use already existing waste management and processing facilities.

Avoiding criticality by limiting the quantity of fissile material in a process system is usually not economical because the minimum critical mass of pure $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ is only $520\mathrm{g}$ for an optimized aqueous system.
This implies that the system at any given time must contain $< 520\mathrm{g}$ of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ .

The addition of neutron absorbers can prevent nuclear criticality, but this action requires full control of the chemistry to avoid the potential for separating the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ from the neutron absorber during the process.

Nuclear Waste Technical Review Board (NWTRB), the Congressionally mandated review board for the proposed Yucca Mountain (YM) geological repository, has also recommended considering the use of DU to isotopically dilute fissile uranium materials to prevent the potential for nuclear criticality in geological repositories containing fissile material (NWTRB 1996).
Finally, a recent NRC report made similar recommendations on the use of DU for criticality control in various disposal facilities (NRC June 1997).

The environmental impact statement (EIS) (DOE June 1996a) and record of decision (DOE July 29, 1996) for the disposition of excess HEU recommended isotopic dilution of the fissile $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ if any HEU was disposed of as a waste.
The previous considerations, as they were applied to $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ , were the basis for this decision.
This precedent suggests that a similar criticality strategy may be used for any $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ that becomes waste.

To ensure control of nuclear criticality for $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ by isotopically diluting $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ with $^{238}\mathrm{U}$ , the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ concentration must be reduced to $< 0.66 \, \mathrm{wt\%}$ (Elam November 1997).

In terms of nuclear criticality safety, this concentration is equivalent to $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ at an enrichment level of $\sim 1.0 \, \mathrm{wt\%}$ -a level which will not result in nuclear criticality under conditions found in processing or disposal facilities.
These uranium isotopic concentrations avoid the need to control other parameters to prevent nuclear criticality; that is, the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ can be treated as just another radioactive waste.

For mixtures of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ and $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ , the amount of DU (with 0.2 wt % $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ ) in grams (g) required to ensure criticality control by isotopic dilution in a water-moderated system is the following:

For historical reasons, $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ disposal was not included in the enabling legislation for many disposal sites.
Consequently, there are a set of legal issues and constraints in terms of disposal of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ as a waste material after it has been converted into a non-weapons-usable form.
If the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ is mixed with another more hazardous waste, the technical and legal requirements for that waste will usually control the final disposition.

For example, if the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ is mixed with DU, converted into reactor fuel, irradiated in a nuclear reactor, and discharged from the reactor as SNF, the legal definition and requirements for disposal of the SNF would apply.
The fact that the fuel contains $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ is not important in this context.
DOE currently owns SNF containing $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ , which is being treated for disposal as SNF-not as a $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ waste form.
There are several other similar options such as blending $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ with high-level waste (HLW) (Sect.
4.1) or blending $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ with TRUW (Sect.
Coprocessing of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ with a waste does not necessarily require mixing of the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ with the waste on an atomic scale.

For example, it is proposed to dispose of excess plutonium by (1) converting the plutonium to a high-grade ceramic, (2) placing the ceramic in cans, (3) placing the small cans in empty HLW canisters, and (4) pouring HLW glass around the cans while filling the HLW canister.

The NRC (Paperiello January 25, 1999) has recently stated that "the staff is not aware of any existing legal or regulatory provisions that would prevent disposal of immobilized plutonium waste forms in a high-level waste repository." This precedent indicates codisposal of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ with various HLW or SNF forms is potentially viable.
Several disposition options for $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ include isotopic dilution of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ with DU followed by disposal of the material as a waste to a WIPP-type disposal facility.
The primary hazard of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ is alpha radiation; thus, WIPP is technically suitable for accepting $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ wastes.
Historically, $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ wastes have been treated as TRUWs.
Congress October 30, 1992) defined WIPP for disposal of TRUW-elements with atomic numbers above 92 (uranium).
This does not include $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ .
If $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ is to be ultimately disposed of as a $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ waste in WIPP, changes in the enabling legislation will be required if the material is sent to WIPP.
Congress 1996), WIPP is authorized to receive and dispose of $175,600\mathrm{m}^3$ of TRUW generated from defense operations.
DOE, in the EIS for WIPP (September 1997), has determined that another $142,000\mathrm{m}^3$ of wastes may require disposal by the year 2033 in a geologic disposal facility such as WIPP.
The other wastes include existing wastes containing ${}^{233}\mathrm{U}$ that are in storage-primarily at ORNL and INEEL (Forsberg, Storch, and Lewis July 7, 1998).

Because of the $142,000\mathrm{m}^3$ of other wastes that ultimately will require disposal, either (1) a second WIPP-type facility must be built, (2) WIPP must be expanded with congressional authorization to accept these other materials, or (3) some new option must be identified.

As a consequence, there are effectively two WIPP-type disposal facility options with different constraints: (1) use the existing WIPP after changes in the enabling legislation allow the disposal of ${}^{233}\mathrm{U}$ wastes from disposition activities or (2) store any future ${}^{233}\mathrm{U}$ disposition waste designed for WIPP-type disposal with existing ${}^{233}\mathrm{U}$ -containing wastes awaiting a disposal facility.
Uranium-233 may be disposed of as a stand-alone $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ waste in the proposed HLW-SNF repository.
Uranium-233 mixed with DU is chemically similar to LWR SNF-uranium dioxide in metal cylinders.

Consequently, adding a few hundred additional drums of waste of isotopically diluted $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ to this existing inventory of several thousand drums would not significantly increase the existing or future waste management activities or costs.

As discussed earlier, the United States does not currently have a disposal site that can accept $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ wastes.

For this reason, existing $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ wastes are managed to meet WIPP WACs.

If weapons-usable $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ is to be converted into a waste, it should meet the equivalent of WIPP WACs to avoid creating a new unique waste form that would require development of special storage facilities and, in the long-term, development of special disposal facilities.

The option does allow the recovery of $^{229}\mathrm{Th}$ for medical applications before disposal of the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ .

This variant requires adding an ion-exchange column for $^{229}\mathrm{Th}$ recovery between (a) the dissolution step and (b) adjusting the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ feed for acceptance by the HLW tanks farm.
To minimize \( ^{233}\mathrm{U} \) processing time and, hence, \( ^{233}\mathrm{U} \) operational costs, it is desirable to add the \( ^{233}\mathrm{U} \) to the minimum amount of HLW.

To minimize HLW glass production, the \( ^{233}\mathrm{U} \) should be mixed with sufficient HLW sludge such that no clean DU or other neutron absorber for criticality control needs to be added to the waste.

Dilution of \( ^{233}\mathrm{U} \) with added DU or other neutron absorbers is to be minimized because added DU or other neutron absorbers generate added HLW glass logs at a cost of \( \\(0.5 - 2 \times 10^{6} / \log \) (processing and disposal costs).
For example, at SRS there are three HLW tanks (26, 33, and 34) with \( 55,000 \) kg of DU.

Adding \( ^{233}\mathrm{U} \) to a small number of tanks is substantially simpler and cheaper than spreading the \( ^{233}\mathrm{U} \) among many tanks with each tank containing only small quantities of \( ^{233}\mathrm{U} \) and DU.

The $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ to be sent to the HLW tanks could be processed into a form that meets waste tank acceptance criteria using (1) existing facilities such as the canyons near the HLW tanks or (2) prepared elsewhere with direct transfer from a shipping cask to the tank farm.
4.4) may also be used to prepare the feed.
This option, like all co-processing options, does impose schedule constraints on the disposition of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ .
The $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ must be in the tanks when the HLW sludge containing the DU is to be processed.
A baseline option (Forsberg October 31, 1997) has been defined for disposition of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ by mixing with HLW sludge and conversion of the sludge into HLW glass at SRS.
The HLW sludge is recovered from the tanks containing the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ and the DU.
The uranium ( $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ and DU) remains with the sludge.
This batch of feed allows the DWPF to operate up to 3 years producing a single type of qualified HLW glass.

This mixing also assures complete mixing of the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ with the DU with elimination of the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ as a weapons-usable material and minimization of long-term criticality issues.
For the previous case, it is estimated that $5\mathrm{L}$ of added HLW glass will be produced per kilogram of ${}^{233}\mathrm{U}$ .

About $1.9\mathrm{L}$ of glass are produced per kilogram ${}^{233}\mathrm{U}$ , assuming that the glass density is $2.5\mathrm{g/cm^3}$ , the waste loading in the glass is $25\mathrm{wt\%}$ , and the chemical form of the uranium in the glass is equivalent to uranium trioxide $(\mathrm{UO}_3)$ .
SRS is currently planning to dispose of limited quantities of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ in its HLW tanks.
This establishes some precedent for disposition of larger quantities of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ in the HLW tanks.
The SRS has $\sim 50$ kg of irradiated targets containing $\sim 50$ g of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ .
It was originally planned to process these targets to recover the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ .
When a surplus of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ developed, the targets were placed in storage.
The targets have degraded with time and were identified in the DNFSB Recommendation 94-1 (May 26, 1994) as a potential safety concern.

To address this safety issue, DOE has decided (DOE October 1995; DOE December 12, 1995; and DOE February 8, 1996) to dissolve the targets in nitric acid in 2003, add sodium hydroxide to the solution to create a slurry, and pump the slurry to the tank farm for eventual conversion to HLW glass.
The basic process for irradiated targets is identical to that proposed for disposition of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ , but there are differences.
For larger quantities of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ , the economics of HLW glass vitrification create strong incentives to minimize additional HLW glass production.
The separated $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ inventory is in a variety of containers-many of which can not be directly fed to the existing SRS equipment.

In this context, processing the separated $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ in inventory will either require modification of one of the existing dissolvers or installation of a small special dissolver in one of the hot cells associated with one of the canyon facilities.
The $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ must be disposed of when the HLW with the DU is being processed into glass.
For the SRS option defined previously, it must be confirmed that the gadolinium and $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ will not separate in the chemical operations that occur in the tank farm and DWPF.

text: Isotopic dilution of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ with DU in a uranium-aluminum alloy forlong-term storage or disposal.
The melt-dilute technology is currently under development at SRS (April 1997) for processing aluminum-clad SNF.
The technology, without modification, can be used to isotopically dilute $^{232}\mathrm{U}$ for long-term storage or prepare a waste form for disposal.
Initially, the incoming SNF or $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ is characterized to determine its uranium content.
This data would be used to determine the amount of DU needed to dilute the HEU or $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ to make the material non-weapons-usable and to address nuclear criticality issues.
The SNF element or $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ would be placed in a crucible and melted.
The final product would be 9 to 67 wt % uranium in the uranium-aluminum alloy.
For example, if the feed contains $\mathrm{U}_3\mathrm{O}_8$ , the following chemical reaction is expected to occur (Adams, Peacock, and Rhode September 9, 1998):
5 2 \mathrm {A l} + 3 \mathrm {U} _ {3} \mathrm {O} _ {8} \rightarrow 8 \mathrm {A l} _ {2} \mathrm {O} _ {3} + 9 \mathrm {U A l} _ {4}. \tag {3}
Three final alloy compositions are being studied at SRS for the uranium-aluminum alloy: 13.2, 30, and 67 wt % uranium.
The final densities of these compositions are about $3.02\mathrm{g/cm^3}$ (measured), $3.64\mathrm{g/cm^3}$ (estimated), and $6.36\mathrm{g/cm^3}$ (estimated), respectively.
The 13.2 wt % composition is the eutectic for the mixture, which results in the lowest melting point (about $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ).
The other two mixtures have higher melting points: about $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for the 30 wt % mixture and about $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for the 67 wt % mixture.
${}^{a}$ Dilution of the ${}^{233}\mathrm{U}$ to a nonweapons enrichment (~12 wt % ${}^{233}\mathrm{U}$ in ${}^{238}\mathrm{U}$ ).

Initial testing of the melt-dilute technology is currently being performed at SRS to determine the viability of the process (Barlow September 9, 1998; Peacock, Adams, and Iyer September 9, 1998; Adams, Peacock, and Rhode September 9, 1998; Krupa September 9, 1998).
It is expected that by mid 1999, DOE will decide whether to proceed to engineering design of the melt-dilute technology.
This option is technically suitable for clean and CEUSP $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ .
The option is not viable for the LWBR $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ .
The LWBR material contains $\sim 350\mathrm{kg}$ of $^{233}\mathrm{UO}_2$ in about 14 t of $\mathrm{ThO}_2$ .
Unless the thorium is converted to metal, the aluminum can not reach the $^{233}\mathrm{UO}_2$ inside the $\mathrm{ThO}_2$ matrix to convert it to metal and dissolve it into aluminum.
Consideration is being given to recover $^{229}\mathrm{Th}$ from the clean and CEUSP $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ for medical purposes.

The same process chemistry that prohibits use of this process on LWBR $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ may allow recovery of $^{229}\mathrm{Th}$ from the clean and CEUSP $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ inventories when the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ is being isotopically diluted with DU.
In these two feeds, the thorium content is measured in parts per million $^{229}\mathrm{Th}$ not natural thorium $^{232}\mathrm{Th}$ .

As the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ oxides are converted to uranium metal producing aluminum oxide, it would be expected that the thorium oxide would be incorporated into the aluminum oxide to produce a slag on top of the metal alloy.
The CEUSP $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ contains significant quantities of gadolinium oxide $(\mathrm{Gd}_2\mathrm{O}_3)$ and cadmium oxide that were added to assure criticality control.
The cadmium oxide is expected to be reduced to metal (Chellew and Bennett 1961).
The $\mathrm{Gd}_2\mathrm{O}_3$ may remain as an oxide (Chellew, Bennett, and Trice 1961) and would be expected to float to the top of the melt and form a slag with other oxide materials.
The packaging of the CEUSP $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ presents significant problems.

The $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ is in the form of an oxide monolith that is integral with the container; i.e., if the container is cut open, the uranium oxides do not flow out of the container but rather remain attached to the walls.
4.3) and borate fusion melt (Sect.
4.4) processes can dissolve the uranium oxides out of the storage cans.
Studies at SRS indicate that both 304 and 316 stainless steel dissolve in molten aluminum with the dissolution rate increasing rapidly with temperature.
Development tests would be required to determine if a practical process could be developed to dissolve the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ and its storage can.
This volume factor is an issue for $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ disposition but it is not an issue for SNF disposition.
The success of the SNF program will determine which options are potentially viable for $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ disposition.

There are three technical challenges for the SNF program: (1) develop a workable uranium-aluminum melt process, (2) develop an off-gas system for the fission products from melting SNF, and (3) qualify the uranium-aluminum alloy for disposal at YM.
The $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ variants are:
If the SNF R&D program is successful and a decision is made to build an SNF process facility; then, the same facility may be used to process $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ and dispose of the waste at YM.
There is an additional uncertainty associated with CEUSP $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ if the final product is sent to YM.
- Build $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ process facility and dispose of waste at YM.
If the R&D program is fully successful, the option exists to build a separate facility for $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ disposition.
This may be a practical option because a $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ uranium-aluminum process in an existing facility would cost a small fraction of what an SNF treatment facility would cost.
With $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ , there does not exist a high-volume, expensive-to-treat, corrosive off-gas.
- Build $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ process facility and send the product to storage or dispose of the material as a waste at a WIPP-type facility.
The presence of cadmium in the CEUSP $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ would not be an issue since RCRA materials are accepted in WIPP-type facilities.

The aqueous nitrate blending process can be used to convert $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ into nonweapons material for long-term storage or into a waste form meeting all technical requirements for disposal at a YM- or WIPP-type facility.
The option is summarized in Table 4.4 and shown in Fig.
It is the most versatile $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ process and is the historical process used to purify and produce various $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ compounds.
It also allows separation of thorium or cadmium compounds from the feed to produce desired storage or disposal forms for $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ -containing materials.

In the aqueous nitrate blending process (ORNL July 13, 1995b), solid ${}^{233}\mathrm{U}$ material is dissolved in nitric acid to produce an aqueous uranyl nitrate solution, which is mixed with a uranyl nitrate solution of DU.
After mixing, the solution is denitrated (by heating) to form an oxide powder from which the ${}^{233}\mathrm{U}$ cannot be separated from the ${}^{238}\mathrm{U}$ without isotopic enrichment.
Alternatively, the solution may also be denitrated in the storage can to produce an oxide monolith similar to the CEUSP ${}^{233}\mathrm{U}$ monoliths.
If desired, thorium, cadmium, and other impurities in the ${}^{233}\mathrm{U}$ feed can be removed by adding a purification step after acid dissolution.
Uranium-233 material is lifted from the storage well into a shielded cask, which is then transported to the loading port at the manipulator hot cell.

Granular or powdered material containing $1\mathrm{kg}^{233}\mathrm{U}$ is converted to a uranyl nitrate solution by reaction of about $3\mathrm{L}$ of $4M$ nitric acid per kilogram of U (as oxide) to produce $\sim 330\mathrm{g}$ U/L of solution.
Dissolution of the thorium oxide- $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ oxide fuel currently stored at INEEL will require the addition of hydrofluoric acid and aluminum nitrate to the nitric acid to aid in dissolution.
Blending is done by mixing measured amounts of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ nitrate solution and depleted uranyl nitrate solution to produce controlled isotopic concentrations of uranium.

To produce a free-flowing powder during denitration, 2 mol of ammonium nitrate per mole of uranium is mixed with the uranyl nitrate solution during the blending step (the modified direct denitration process).
The powder has a low density (about $1\mathrm{g/cm^3}$ ) and is in a form that might be easily dispersed.
Several methods are available to consolidate the material to a safer monolithic storage form (Table 4.5).
The powder can be compacted to $4.3\mathrm{g/cm^3}$ (about $60\%$ of the theoretical density of $\mathrm{UO}_3$ ) to produce a nondusty, monolithic form.
Alternatively, the oxide may be incorporated into a cement grout at a $50\mathrm{wt}\%$ loading.
${}^{a}$ The required isotopic dilution for criticality control is 0.66 wt $\% {}^{233}\mathrm{U}$ in pure ${}^{238}\mathrm{U}$ .
If DU with ${0.2}\mathrm{{wt}}\% {}^{235}\mathrm{U}$ is used,the final concentration of ${}^{233}\mathrm{U}$ is ${0.53}\mathrm{{wt}}\%$ .
Some of the ${}^{238}\mathrm{U}$ must be used for criticality control of the ${}^{235}\mathrm{U}$ in the DU.
The solution is slowly dripped into the product can that is in a vertical tube furnace at a temperature of $\sim 800^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
There are some technical uncertainties associated with the grout because of the possibility of generation of hydrogen from $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ alpha radiolysis.
The proposed YMR is designed to accept 63,000 metric tons of initial heavy metal SNF in the form of uranium dioxide pellets in metal tubes.

Isotopic dilution of $1\mathrm{kg}$ of ${}^{233}\mathrm{U}$ to non-weapons-grade uranium by the aqueous-nitrate-blending process will produce $10.1\mathrm{kg}$ of $\mathrm{UO}_3$ (8.407 kg U) as pellets having a volume of 2.3 L.
Grouting the oxide will produce $20.2\mathrm{kg}$ of material having a volume of 4.8 L.
In-can denitration will produce $9.9\mathrm{kg}$ of $\mathrm{U}_3\mathrm{O}_8$ product having a volume of 3.1 L.
Blending $1\mathrm{kg}$ of ${}^{233}\mathrm{U}$ to eliminate long-term criticality will produce $227\mathrm{kg}$ of $\mathrm{UO}_3$ pellets (189 kg U) having a volume of 52.8 L.
Incorporation of the oxide into grout will produce $454\mathrm{kg}$ of grout with a volume of $108.2\mathrm{L/kg}$ of ${}^{233}\mathrm{U}$ .
Denitrification of the blended nitrate solution in the product can will produce $223\mathrm{kg}$ of $\mathrm{U}_3\mathrm{O}_8$ having a volume of 67.4 L.

The actual waste volume per kilogram of ${}^{233}\mathrm{U}$ will be higher in many cases because other materials present in some of the ${}^{233}\mathrm{U}$ feeds (thorium oxide, cadmium oxides, gadolinium oxides, etc.).
Uranium-233 and DU can be converted to glass by dissolution of uranium oxides into molten glass formers in a high temperature furnace.
There are two process variants; one chosen to minimize costs (Table 4.6; Fig.
4.6) and the other chosen to produce a high quality waste glass that would meet YM-type waste form requirements (Table 4.7; Fig.
It can be used to isotopically dilute $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ oxides with DU oxides and create a solid borate fusion melt suitable for long-term storage or disposal at a WIPP-type facility.
The process could be used to dilute $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ to 12 wt $\%$ $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ in $^{238}\mathrm{U}$ (nonweapons $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ ) that is stored for potential future use.
The product characteristics would allow relatively simple recovery of uranium or decay products such as $^{229}\mathrm{Th}$ (for medical applications) at a later date.
The process may also be used as a pretreatment step for $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ disposal in HLW glass (Sect.

This process would not be suitable for dilution of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ to 12 wt % $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ in $^{238}\mathrm{U}$ (nonweapons $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ ) that is stored for potential future uses because of the difficulty in recovery of uranium from such a glass.

The respective densities of ${\mathrm{B}}_{2}{\mathrm{O}}_{3},{\mathrm{{UO}}}_{3}$ ,and a fusion melt with ${80}\mathrm{{wt}}\% {\mathrm{{UO}}}_{3}$ (estimated) are ${2.46}\mathrm{\;g}/{\mathrm{{cm}}}^{3},{7.29}\mathrm{\;g}/{\mathrm{{cm}}}^{3}$ ,and ${6.32}\mathrm{\;g}/{\mathrm{{cm}}}^{3}$ .

In all fusion melt processes (ORNL July 27, 1995), the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ oxide is mixed with DU oxide powder and solvent metal oxide powder and melted in an induction furnace to produce a material in which the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ cannot be separated from $^{238}\mathrm{U}$ by chemical or mechanical means.
The use of solvent oxides reduces fusion melt temperatures from $2,500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for pure $\mathrm{UO}_2$ to temperatures between 800 and $1,200^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The loading of uranium oxides may exceed 80 wt%.
In the glass fusion melt process, a glass frit containing alkali oxides and silica is used to produce a high-uranium-content glass with uranium oxide loadings up to 50 wt%.
The $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ material to be processed is removed from the storage wells and transported in a shielded carrier to the processing hot cell.
The nominal processing rate is $1.2\mathrm{kg}$ of fissile uranium per day, assuming a three-shift-per-day operation and a 4-h cycle per batch using one melt-blend furnace.
After the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ material is converted to an acceptable powder form, it is blended with DU and a specified solvent oxide mix consisting largely of alkali-metal oxides and silica.
The final process temperature will be adjusted to achieve complete melting for the type $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ product glass being processed.

The isotopic dilution of $1\mathrm{kg}$ of ${}^{233}\mathrm{U}$ to non-weapons-grade uranium by the dry melt process will produce $\sim 19.8\mathrm{kg}$ of oxide product (containing $8.407\mathrm{kg}$ of U and $9.9\mathrm{kg}$ of alkali metal oxide and silica) having a volume of $5\mathrm{L}$ .

Blending $1\mathrm{kg}$ of ${}^{233}\mathrm{U}$ to eliminate long-term criticality will produce $446\mathrm{kg}$ of oxide ( $189\mathrm{kg}$ U as $\mathrm{U}_3\mathrm{O}_8$ and $214.4\mathrm{kg}$ of alkali metal oxide and silica) with a volume of $115.5\mathrm{L}$ .
The actual waste volume per kilogram of ${}^{233}\mathrm{U}$ will be higher for those feeds containing impurities (thorium oxide, cadmium, and gadolinium oxides).
The option may minimize or eliminate the need to grind the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ feed materials.
Second, the process may be applicable to LWBR $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ .

The CEUSP $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ was solidified inside its stainless steel container and is partly attached to the container walls.
Removal of the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ from the container would be a complex, expensive, mechanical operation.

The $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ container would be cut into several pieces and dumped into the liner, the furnace would be heated to operating temperature, and the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ would be dissolved into the molten mixture from the container pieces.

After dissolution of the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ , additional DU would be added to the furnace, the dissolution process would be completed, the melt would be cooled, and the solid product (with container parts) would be packaged as one piece.

Additional studies and thermodynamic and chemical kinetic data on uranium dissolution in fusion melts is required to predict (1) processing time and (2) if grinding of the feed is required in the process.

section: 4.5 DRY GRINDING, BLENDING, AND SINTERING (STORAGE AND DISPOSAL)
text: However, the product requirements for the dry-blend option are much less than those required for fuel fabrication; thus, simplification of the process should be possible.The process produces very fine 233U particulates (~0.5 μm) that are a significant inhalation hazard.
The $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ to be processed (ORNL July 19, 1995) is removed from the storage wells and transported in a shielded carrier to the processing hot cell.
The nominal processing rate is $1.2\mathrm{kg}$ of fissile uranium per day, assuming an operation of 3 shifts per day and a 4-h cycle per batch.
The batch size is chosen to be $200\mathrm{g}$ to ensure criticality control.
Blending begins by adding a weighed amount of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ oxide powder with a predetermined quantity of DU dioxide powder in a mixing vessel.

The ensuing pellets are to be limited to a mass of $57\mathrm{g}$ or less and a maximum diameter or height of $3\mathrm{cm}$ , and the nominal fissionable material in each pellet will not exceed $1\mathrm{g}$ .
The finished pellets will be $\sim 90\%$ of theoretical density $(9.86~\mathrm{g / cm^3}$ for $\mathrm{UO}_2)$ of the mixed oxide.

Isotopic dilution of $1\mathrm{kg}$ of ${}^{233}\mathrm{U}$ by the dry-blend process will produce $9.5\mathrm{kg}$ of non-weapons-usable $\mathrm{UO}_2$ ( $8.407\mathrm{kg}$ U) product having a pellet volume of $\sim 0.96\mathrm{L}$ .

Blending $1\mathrm{kg}$ of ${}^{233}\mathrm{U}$ with DU to eliminate long-term criticality will produce $214\mathrm{kg}$ of uranium oxide ( $189\mathrm{kg}$ U) product having a pellet volume of $\sim 21.7\mathrm{L}$ .

However, the actual waste volume per kilogram ${}^{233}\mathrm{U}$ will be higher because of the presence of other material (thorium, cadmium, and gadolinium oxides) in the ${}^{233}\mathrm{U}$ material, any additional DU required to dilute ${}^{235}\mathrm{U}$ contained in the ${}^{233}\mathrm{U}$ material, and any DU added to accommodate measurement uncertainties.

One option being examined for dry down-blending of HEU with DU consists of grinding and mixing $^{235}\mathrm{UO}_3$ and $\mathrm{DUO}_3$ in a dual drive planetary mill with alumina ball grinding media for $\sim 75$ minutes.
The grinding media remains with the uranium oxides and occupies $\sim 35$ vol % of the final product.
Currently, it is uncertain whether powder mixing of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ and DU in the same chemical form assures isotopic irreversible mixing.
There is almost a $2\%$ difference in the densities of $^{233}\mathrm{UO}_2$ and $^{238}\mathrm{UO}_2$ .
At small particle sizes ( $< 1\mu \mathrm{m}$ ), there is the potential of self-separation by alpha-initiated Brownian motion.
With small particulates, the recoil from alpha decay can move the $^{233}\mathrm{UO}_2$ particle.

In a mixture of $^{233}\mathrm{UO}_2$ and $^{238}\mathrm{UO}_2$ particulates, the lighter $^{233}\mathrm{UO}_2$ with its alpha-driven motion may preferentially work its way to the top of a powder mixture.

This type of potential separation mechanism does not exist when mixing $^{235}\mathrm{UO}_2$ and $\mathrm{DUO}_2$ powders to create LEU because of the relatively low radioactivity of each uranium isotope.
The current waste form requirements (DOE, August 30, 1994) prohibit waste forms containing fine particulates.

Oxidation and dissolution studies (Hanson July 1998; McEachern and Taylor 1998) have shown that the addition of rare-earth oxides-such as gadolinium oxide-to $\mathrm{UO}_2$ significantly alters $\mathrm{UO}_2$ oxidation and dissolution rates under a wide variety of conditions.
The requirement that the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ and DU have the same chemical form applies to the LWBR material.
However, the LWBR feed contains only 1 to 12 wt % $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ in high-fired $\mathrm{ThO}_2$ and must be matched to a comparable feed.
The DU (for blending purposes) would contain 1-12 wt % DU in high-fired $\mathrm{ThO}_2$ .
The LWBR $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ contains $\sim 350\mathrm{kg}$ of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ incorporated in 14 t of $\mathrm{ThO}_2$ .
To isotopically dilute the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ to non-weaponsusable material would require DU in about 120 t of $\mathrm{ThO}_2$ .
To eliminate criticality concerns, over 2,000 t of $\mathrm{ThO}_2$ with DU would be required.

If the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ feed has variable or complex chemical compositions (CEUSP and LWBR $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ ), dry blending with high-temperature sintering does allow the use of normal DU oxides to isotopically dilute the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ .
Fine grains of $\mathrm{DUO}_2$ and the $^{233}\mathrm{UO}_2$ diffuse together into larger single crystals.

In the fabrication of LWBR fuel pellets, $\mathrm{ThO}_2$ and $^{233}\mathrm{UO}_2$ powders were blending, pressed into pellets, and sintered at high temperatures until the thorium and uranium interdiffuse to create a homogeneous Th-U oxide.
This does require fine-powder blending ( $\sim 0.5\mu \mathrm{m}$ ).

One option for the disposition of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ is to use it in the fabrication of LWR fuel and its subsequent irradiation in a commercial nuclear reactor.
To effect this option, the surplus $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ would be blended with either DU or enriched, commercial-grade uranium to produce suitable fuel material.

The United States has experience in the manufacture and irradiation of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ -based fuels.
It was started up in 1957, was shut down in 1982, and was decommissioned over the ensuing few years (DOE July 1992).
Shippingport was initially an enriched $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ -fueled pressurized LWR, but it was converted in 1977 to an LWBR based on the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ -thorium fuel cycle.

To minimize the radiological dose to workers during Shippingport fuel fabrication, the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ nitrate solution, processed at ORNL, was purified by ion exchange to remove the $^{232}\mathrm{U}$ decay products and to provide a window of time during which the radiation field from the material produced was significantly reduced.

The resulting solution was then converted to oxide and shipped, not more than 1 week to 10 d following purification, to Bettis Atomic Power Laboratory for fuel fabrication in a relatively low radiation field.
It is noteworthy that the largest radiation exposure of personnel under the Naval Reactors Program occurred in 1975.

The combined cumulative dose to all navy program personnel was 15 times greater than the amount reported in 1994-primarily because of the fabrication of the LWBR core for Shippingport (Mangeno and Burrows March 1995).

Some of the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ in inventory today is of better quality than the Shippingport material; however, a significant quantity of the current inventory has higher $^{232}\mathrm{U}$ impurities and higher radiation levels than the material that was processed.
The experience gained from handling $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ materials indicates that control of radiation doses is the major issue.

For purposes of comparison, the alpha activity for the scenarios analyzed is estimated from the amounts of ${}^{232}\mathrm{U}$ , ${}^{233}\mathrm{U}$ , ${}^{234}\mathrm{U}$ , ${}^{236}\mathrm{U}$ , and their decay products.

The addition of the Y-12 ${}^{233}\mathrm{U}$ to the SRS LEU increases the alpha activity only slightly, while the alpha activity is increased by about a factor of 5 with the addition of the CEUSP ${}^{233}\mathrm{U}$ and about a factor of 10 with the addition of all ${}^{233}\mathrm{U}$ -containing materials.
The alpha activity varies with time, primarily because of the decay of ${}^{232}\mathrm{U}$ and the ingrowth of ${}^{232}\mathrm{U}$ daughters.
For a material initially containing only ${}^{232}\mathrm{U}$ , the alpha activity will peak after about 10 years of decay.

It should be noted that the alpha activity for typical HEU is about $2.38 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{~Bq/kg}$ U (Duerksen May 19, 1997) and is about $2.68 \times 10^{12} \mathrm{~Bq/kg}$ Pu for WGP (Albright, Berkhout, and Walker 1997).
A unique feature of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ is that it contains $^{232}\mathrm{U}$ .
The $^{232}\mathrm{U}$ contaminant in $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ can result in a large gamma activity and dose.
A daughter of $^{232}\mathrm{U}$ , $^{208}\mathrm{Tl}$ , emits a 2.6 MeV gamma with a half-life of about 3 min.
Similar to the alpha activity, the gamma activity will peak after about 10 years of decay.
The concentration of the $^{232}\mathrm{U}$ determines the gamma activity and ultimately determines the potential dose to workers.
The $^{232}\mathrm{U}$ content of the proposed blend of SRS LEU and Y-12 HEU would be well under the specification.
However, for the CEUSP and all $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ -containing materials blends, the $^{232}\mathrm{U}$ specification would be exceeded.
Of the three scenarios considered, only the Y-12 $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ appears to be a viable candidate material for disposition with the SRS off-specification HEU as LWR fuel.

The resulting blend would be almost indistinguishable from the proposed SRS LEU blend and should have no significant impact on operations because of its unique characteristics (i.e., it is primarily HEU with a small amount of $^{232}\mathrm{U}$ and $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ ).
No legal barrier has been identified that would prevent the blending of the Y-12 $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ with the off-specification HEU and DU.
For the CEUSP and all $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ -containing materials, the ASTM specifications are significantly exceeded for both $^{232}\mathrm{U}$ content and alpha activity.

Before their processing, these materials could be purified by removing the $^{232}\mathrm{U}$ decay products, thereby reducing radiation levels and thus affording a window of opportunity for processing the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ in a lower radiation field.
However, because the $^{232}\mathrm{U}$ decay products would build up rather rapidly, such an option would require close coordination and strict schedules.

It is unlikely that it would be possible to purify the material (by removing $^{232}\mathrm{U}$ decay products), blend the resulting material with the off-specification HEU and DU, convert the blend to oxide, fabricate pellets and fuel, and install the fuel assembly in a reactor before the radiation levels increased significantly.
Therefore, the CEUSP $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ and all $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ -containing materials are not viable options for blending with the SRS off-specification HEU.

The deep-borehole concept for geologic disposal of excess $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ -bearing material includes the emplacement of the excess material into the lower part of one or more underground boreholes, each of which is drilled into a stable rock formation.

Application of deep boreholes as a disposition option for excess WGP was studied extensively by LLNL and is documented in Alternative Technical Summary Report for Immobilized Disposition in Deep Boreholes, UCRL-LR-121736 (Wijesinghe et al.
If the borehole disposition concept is adopted, $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ -bearing materials would be emplaced and sealed in a region of stable rock that lies 2 to $4\mathrm{km}$ below the surface.
August 23, 1996), the deep borehole-emplacement depths can be several thousands of meters greater than those of mined geologic repositories.

At such depths, the groundwater is expected to be relatively stagnant and have temperatures of 75 to $150^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , pressures of 7,500 to 15,000 psi, and dissolved salt content of up to 40 wt %.
Two alternatives have been identified for the deep-borehole disposal option (Wijesinghe et. al August 23, 1996): immobilized disposition and direct disposition.
Figure 4.20 illustrates the concept for the immobilized disposal of coated ceramic pellets in grout.
For most of the domestic $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ inventory, little material conversion and some repackaging would be necessary.
Consequently, the appropriate application of the deep borehole option for any excess $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ -bearing materials would be the direct disposition alternative.
A borehole facility for the disposition of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ -bearing materials would be another federal waste geologic disposal facility that would require public review and approval for licensing.
However, there are significant hurdles to clear regarding politics, public approval, and licensing before the deep borehole option could be implemented for $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ -bearing materials.
It is not a practical option for the small quantities of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ .
The Nuclear Waste Policy Act (1982) and the Nuclear Waste Policy Amendments Act (1987) stipulate the requirements for disposing of radioactive wastes.
